32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown
41	# DAG Walker Directive
42	
43	## Slug
44	<slug>
45	
46	## Seed
47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49	
50	## Strategy context
51	<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted — what this cone supports in
52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
53	
54	## Depth / scope
55	<how far up to walk: "the whole cone to the axioms" (default), or "one level —
56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```
62	
63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	   **When the node has Lean code, the Lean side is the ground truth.** Read the matched `.lean` declaration (you read Lean; you never edit it): the facts its proof *actually* invokes — project lemmas it calls, instances it needs — are what `\uses{}` must transcribe, not just what the informal sketch happens to mention. And the project keeps a **1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence**: if the cone's Lean files contain helper declarations with no blueprint entry (`archon dag-query unmatched` lists them), give each one an entry — trivial statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}`, accurate `\uses{}`, one-line proof — and wire its consumers to `\uses{}` it. A helper without tex is an invisible dependency.
87	
88	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
89	
90	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
91	
92	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
93	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
94	
95	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
96	
97	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait for it, open the new `references/<slug>.md`, then write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
98	
99	## What you must / must not do
100	
101	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
102	- **Respect protected material** (the "Protected by the mathematician" section of your invocation prompt): never edit a protected chapter or an `all`-protected label's block; a `statement`-protected block may only gain a `proof` environment. Wiring INTO protected blocks is fine — other declarations may `\uses{}` their labels — but their own `\uses{}` lists are the mathematician's; report missing edges there in "Notes for dispatcher" instead of editing.
103	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
104	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
105	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
106	
107	## Workflow
108	
109	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
110	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
111	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
112	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
113	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
114	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
115	7. Write your report.

40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

31	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
32	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
33	
34	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
35	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
36	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
37	equivalently (and functorially) the set
38	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
39	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
40	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
41	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
42	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
43	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
44	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).
45

69	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) be the identity component of the

76	  the genus \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)
77	  (\cref{def:genus}).

91	% \(a\mapsto a+0\cdot\ve\).  Hence taking cohomology yields this split exact
92	% sequence of Abelian groups:
93	%    \(\)0\to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}) \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*)
94	%     \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}^*) \to 1.\(\)"
95	% "In Square (\ref{CD:tgtsp}), the two vertical maps are isomorphisms by
96	% Exercise~\ref{ex:Alr} since \(\IPic_{X/k}\) represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\) and
97	% \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, \(v\)
98	% too is an isomorphism, as desired."
99	\begin{proof}
100	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
101	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
102	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~5.11. By
103	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, the Picard scheme
104	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists and represents the étale-sheafified relative
105	  Picard functor \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\); the identity component
106	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
107	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}), so the tangent
108	  spaces at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
109	  agree; we may therefore work with \(\Pic_{C/k}\) throughout.
110	
111	  \emph{Tangent space via dual numbers.} Let
112	  \(k_\varepsilon := k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\) be the ring of
113	  dual numbers. For any \(k\)-scheme \(P\) locally of finite type and
114	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in P(k)\), the Zariski tangent space
115	  \(\tu T_e P\) is in functorial bijection with the set
116	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-valued points whose
117	  underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). When \(P\) is a \(k\)-group scheme,
118	  the natural homomorphism \(k_\varepsilon \to k\) (\(\varepsilon
119	  \mapsto 0\)) induces a group homomorphism
120	  \(P(k_\varepsilon) \to P(k)\) whose kernel coincides with
121	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\); under this identification the vector-space
122	  addition on \(\tu T_e P\) corresponds to the group multiplication on
123	  the kernel. Specialising to \(P = \Pic_{C/k}\),
124	  \[
125	    \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}
126	    \;=\; \ker\bigl(\Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) \to \Pic_{C/k}(k)\bigr).
127	  \]
128	
129	  \emph{Computing the kernel via the truncated exponential sequence.}
130	  Set \(C_\varepsilon := C \otimes_k k_\varepsilon\). On the étale
131	  site of \(C\) the truncated exponential sequence of sheaves of
132	  abelian groups
133	  \[
134	    0 \to \mathcal{O}_C \to \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}
135	        \to \mathcal{O}_C^{\times} \to 1,
136	  \]
137	  whose first map sends a local section \(b\) to \(1 + b\varepsilon\),
138	  is split by \(a \mapsto a + 0 \cdot \varepsilon\). Taking sheaf
139	  cohomology produces a split exact sequence
140	  \[
141	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)
142	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times})
143	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \to 1.
144	  \]
145	
146	  \emph{Identification with the tangent space.} Because \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
147	  represents the étale-sheafification of
148	  \(T \mapsto H^1(C_T, \mathcal{O}_{C_T}^{\times})\), there is a
149	  commutative diagram of abelian groups
150	  \[
151	    \begin{array}{ccc}
152	      H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}) &
153	        \longrightarrow & H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \\
154	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
155	      \Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) & \longrightarrow &
156	        \Pic_{C/k}(k),
157	    \end{array}
158	  \]
159	  whose vertical maps are isomorphisms after base change to the
160	  algebraic closure \(\bar k\) (Kleiman~\S 2, Exercise~2.3;
161	  invocation legitimised by
162	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Taking horizontal
163	  kernels yields a \(k\)-linear comparison map
164	  \(v : H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) \to \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}\) which is an
165	  isomorphism after base change to \(\bar k\), hence already an
166	  isomorphism over \(k\) (the source and target are finite-dimensional
167	  \(k\)-vector spaces, and an isomorphism of \(k\)-vector spaces is
168	  detected after a faithfully flat extension).
169	
170	  \emph{Compatibility with the additive structure.} The diagram of the
171	  preceding paragraph is functorial in \(k\)-linear endomorphisms of
172	  \(k_\varepsilon\); in particular scalar multiplication by \(a \in
173	  k\) on the dual numbers corresponds, on
174	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), to scalar multiplication by \(a\) in the
175	  natural \(k\)-module structure. Hence \(v\) is a homomorphism of
176	  \(k\)-vector spaces, as required.
177	
178	  Finally, by the genus definition (Hartshorne~IV.1.1,
179	  \cref{def:genus}), \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\); thus
180	  \(\dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\).
181	\end{proof}

183	\section{Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
184	\label{sec:pic0_smooth}
185	
186	By the tangent-space dimension bound for a Noetherian local ring,
187	\(\dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g\), with
188	equality if and only if \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular at \(0\). For a
189	smooth proper curve, this inequality becomes an equality: smoothness
190	at the identity propagates to smoothness everywhere by
191	translation, hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth \(k\)-group scheme of
192	dimension \(g\).
193

252	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
253	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~5.13, with the curve-specific dimension
254	  input supplied by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}.
255	
256	  Since smoothness of a scheme over \(k\) descends along faithfully
257	  flat extensions, we may replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure
258	  \(\bar k\) (using \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
259	  to identify \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\)).
260	  Over \(\bar k\) every closed point of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
261	  \(\bar k\)-rational, and the group law translates the identity \(0\)
262	  onto an arbitrary closed point: the translation morphism
263	  \(t_\lambda : \Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(x \mapsto x +
264	  \lambda\), is an automorphism by
265	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}. Consequently
266	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) has the same local dimension at \(\lambda\) as at
267	  \(0\), and is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is smooth at
268	  \(0\).
269	
270	  By the standard tangent-space dimension bound, for any Noetherian
271	  local ring \((A, \mathfrak{m})\) with residue field \(k\) one has
272	  \(\dim A \le \dim_k (\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)\), with equality
273	  if and only if \(A\) is regular. Over an algebraically closed field,
274	  regularity at a \(\bar k\)-rational point is equivalent to
275	  smoothness. Hence
276	  \(
277	    \dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)
278	  \)
279	  by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}, with equality iff
280	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular (equivalently, smooth) at \(0\).
281	
282	  It remains to show that for \(C/k\) a smooth proper curve, equality
283	  holds (so smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) follows). For a smooth
284	  proper curve there is no obstruction: as Kleiman~\S 5 Ex.~5.23
285	  notes, projectivity and flatness of \(C/k\) together with the
286	  smoothness of \(C\) over \(k\) imply that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a
287	  smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a(C) =
288	  g(C)\). In characteristic zero this reduces to Cartier's theorem
289	  (Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~5.14): every \(k\)-group scheme locally of
290	  finite type over a field of characteristic zero is smooth; in
291	  positive characteristic the smoothness uses the curve-specific
292	  Hodge-theoretic vanishing
293	  \(H^2(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = 0\) (the source-target of the obstruction
294	  to lifting tangent vectors to first-order deformations), which holds
295	  for any curve since \(C\) has dimension \(1\). In either case
296	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); by the translation argument
297	  above, it is smooth everywhere.
298	
299	  Thus \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth and
300	  \(\dim \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\), completing the proof.
301	\end{proof}

303	\section{Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
304	\label{sec:pic0_proper}
305	
306	A smooth proper geometrically integral curve is geometrically normal,
307	so Kleiman's \(\Pic^0\)-projectivity theorem applies and upgrades the
308	quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
309	projective over \(k\), in particular proper.

316	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}

340	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
341	% L2941-L2984: the proof of th:qpp&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
342	% structure theorem to reduce projectivity to ruling out non-constant
343	% maps from the multiplicative group; the curve case relies on

365	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
366	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
367	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
368	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~5.4, specialised to \(X = C\) a
369	  smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
370	
371	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} By
372	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists,
373	  represents \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), and is locally of finite type; its
374	  identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite type by
375	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}.
376	  Kleiman's quasi-projectivity statement
377	  (\S 5 Thm.~5.4, first conclusion) then gives that
378	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, since \(C/k\)
379	  is projective and geometrically integral.
380	
381	  \emph{Reduction to algebraically closed \(k\).} Properness descends
382	  through faithfully flat extensions of the base field
383	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 2.7.1(vii)), and formation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
384	  commutes with extension of \(k\)
385	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Hence it
386	  suffices to prove that \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k} =
387	  \Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\) is proper, and we may assume
388	  \(k = \bar k\) is algebraically closed throughout.
389	
390	  \emph{Reduction via Chevalley--Rosenlicht.} A quasi-projective
391	  scheme is projective if and only if it is proper, so it suffices to
392	  show that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is proper over \(\bar k\). By the
393	  Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem (Conrad, ``A modern proof of
394	  Chevalley's theorem on algebraic groups'', Thm.~1.1, p.~3), every
395	  reduced connected algebraic \(\bar k\)-group is an extension of an
396	  abelian variety by a linear (affine) algebraic group. The
397	  multiplicative group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) and the additive group
398	  \(\mathbb{G}_a\) generate every connected solvable linear group
399	  (Lie--Kolchin); since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is commutative, hence
400	  solvable, properness will follow once we show that every
401	  \(\bar k\)-morphism \(t : \mathbb{G}_m \to (\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\red}\)
402	  is constant.
403	
404	  \emph{No non-constant map from \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (uses geometric
405	  normality of \(C\)).} The smooth proper curve \(C/\bar k\) is
406	  in particular geometrically normal. Suppose
407	  \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\), where \(T = \mathbb{A}^1
408	  \setminus \{0\}\) is the multiplicative group; by the comparison
409	  theorem (Kleiman~\S 2 Thm.~2.5) \(t\) is represented by an
410	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times T\). Since
411	  \(C \times T\) is normal and integral, every invertible sheaf is the
412	  sheaf of a Cartier divisor: write
413	  \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}_{C \times T}(D)\) for some divisor \(D\)
414	  (Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15). Restricting \(\mathcal{L}\) to the generic
415	  fibre of the projection \(p : C \times T \to C\): the generic fibre
416	  is \(T\) localised at the generic point of \(C\), and an invertible
417	  sheaf on an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{A}^1\) is trivial. Hence
418	  there is a rational function \(\phi\) on \(C \times T\) such that
419	  \((\phi) + D\) restricts to the trivial divisor on the generic
420	  fibre. Pick any section \(s : C \to C \times T\) of \(p\) (e.g.\ at
421	  a closed point of \(T\)), and set \(E := s^*((\phi) + D)\); then
422	  \(E\) is a divisor on \(C\) with the property that \(p^* E\) and
423	  \((\phi) + D\) coincide as cycles, hence as divisors (Altman--Kleiman
424	  AK70, Prp.~3.10, p.~139, valid since \(C \times T\) is normal).
425	  Therefore \(\mathcal{L} \cong p^* \mathcal{O}_C(E)\), so the
426	  associated map \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\) factors through the
427	  generic fibre and is constant. This rules out non-constant maps
428	  \(\mathbb{G}_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\); the additive case
429	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is analogous (and follows from the
430	  multiplicative case via the standard embedding
431	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m\) on solvable
432	  subgroups). Hence the linear part in the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
433	  decomposition of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is trivial, so \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
434	  an abelian variety; in particular, it is proper. Combined with
435	  quasi-projectivity, this yields projectivity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
436	  over \(\bar k\), hence over \(k\) by faithfully flat descent of
437	  properness.
438	\end{proof}

493	\end{proof}
494	
495	\section{Assembly: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety}
496	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
497	
498	A \(k\)-group scheme is an \emph{abelian variety} iff its underlying
499	\(k\)-scheme is a complete (proper) connected group variety
500	(Milne~\S I.1, p.~8). Combining the smoothness, properness, and
501	geometric irreducibility statements of this chapter with the
502	identity-component group-scheme structure of
503	\cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent} packages \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an
504	abelian variety of dimension \(g\). This is the load-bearing A.3.vii
505	gate for Route~A.
506	
507	\begin{theorem}\leanok
508	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
509	  \label{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
510	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.isAbelianVariety}
511	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
512	        thm:pic0_proper,
513	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
514	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
515	
516	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
517	  % \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety) (read from
518	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~14).
519	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
520	  % "A complete connected group variety is called an abelian variety. As we
521	  % shall see, they are projective, and (fortunately) commutative. Their
522	  % group laws will be written additively. Thus \(t_a\) is now denoted
523	  % \(x \mapsto x + a\) and \(e\) is usually denoted \(0\)."
524	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
525	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~51 (read from
526	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3990-L3996).
527	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
528	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
529	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
530	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
531	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
532	  % Exercise~\ref{ex:jac}."
533	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
534	  \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety); [Kleiman], ``The
535	  Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~5.26 (the Jacobian
536	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) of a smooth proper family of curves is a
537	  projective abelian \(S\)-scheme).}
538	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
539	  genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component
540	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is an
541	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)} of dimension \(g\): the underlying
542	  \(k\)-scheme is smooth (\cref{thm:pic0_smooth}), proper
543	  (\cref{thm:pic0_proper}), and geometrically irreducible
544	  (\cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}), and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme
545	  structure inherited from \(\Pic_{C/k}\) via the inclusion
546	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
547	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}). In
548	  particular, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is automatically commutative (Milne~\S
549	  I.1, Cor.~1.4).
550	\end{theorem}
551	
552	\begin{proof}
553	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
554	        thm:pic0_proper,
555	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
556	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
557	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
558	  We assemble Milne's defining axioms of an abelian variety from the
559	  four conjuncts established earlier in this chapter and in
560	  \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}.
561	
562	  \emph{(i) Group-variety structure.} By
563	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism} applied to
564	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
565	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}), the identity component
566	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
567	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) compatible with the clopen inclusion.
568	
569	  \emph{(ii) Smoothness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}, the structural
570	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth (of relative
571	  dimension \(g\)). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth
572	  \(k\)-variety: it is reduced (Milne's standing convention bundles
573	  reducedness into ``variety''), geometrically reduced (by
574	  smoothness over \(k\)), and locally of finite type.
575	
576	  \emph{(iii) Properness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_proper}, the structural
577	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is proper (in fact
578	  projective). This is Milne's ``complete'' axiom.
579	
580	  \emph{(iv) Connectedness (geometric irreducibility).} By
581	  \cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}, the underlying scheme
582	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; in particular it is
583	  geometrically connected, hence connected.
584	
585	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, proper,
586	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme equipped with a
587	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure. By Milne~\S I.1, p.~8, ``a complete
588	  connected group variety is called an abelian variety''. Thus
589	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety over \(k\); commutativity is
590	  automatic (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Its dimension is the relative
591	  dimension of the smooth morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\),
592	  which equals \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) by
593	  \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso} and \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}.
594	
595	  This is the content of Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~5.26: for \(X/S\)
596	  projective and smooth with connected curve fibres of genus
597	  \(g > 0\) (specialise to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\)), the Jacobian
598	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) exists and is a projective abelian
599	  \(S\)-scheme. The genus-zero case is degenerate
600	  (\(\Pic^0_{C/k} = 0\), Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)) but is
601	  consistent with the assembly: a point scheme \(\Spec k\) is
602	  trivially an abelian variety of dimension \(0\).
603	\end{proof}

662	\section{Out of scope}
663	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety_out_of_scope}
